_comment: PHASE C-1 — Fredericksburg (the Marye's Heights assault), Dec 13, 1862, for the __FIELD real-time engine. Second data-driven scenario (the bullrun1 pattern, GAME_DATA.fredericksburg.fredericksburg, registered in src/tactical/T1 fldScenarioRegistry). Models the ICONIC FRONTAL ASSAULT only: Burnside's army storms WEST/uphill from the town across the open glacis toward the SUNKEN TELEGRAPH ROAD, where Longstreet's men crouch behind a 4-foot stone wall at the foot of Marye's Heights, with pre-sighted artillery on the crest. The Union fed seven divisions in piecemeal — some fourteen charges, none reaching the wall (closest ~25-40 yd) — and lost ~8,000 men before it. Modeled as Union assault WAVES vs a walled defense that THICKENS as CS reinforcements crowd up. The southern front (Franklin vs Jackson at Prospect Hill / Meade's breakthrough) is teaching context only, not the playable field. COORDINATE NOTE: the engine field is 1200x900 yards; per engine convention the ATTACKER (US) sits at HIGH z facing -z (facing 0) and the DEFENDER (CS) at LOW z facing +z (facing PI). In THIS field: high z = the town + the Rappahannock (the Union start/rear); a canal/millrace crosses the plain ~z 640 (3 narrow bridges); the open killing glacis is ~z 420-600; the STONE WALL + sunken road front the objective ~z 400; the defenders hold just behind it ~z 355; Marye's Heights + its crest artillery rise at low z ~235. defaultFog is FALSE (the morning fog had lifted by the main assault — the slaughter happened in clear fields of fire: 'a chicken could not live on that field'). Per-brigade strengths are Inferred approximations; the Verified, teaching-bearing facts are the wave order/commanders, the walled-defense geometry, and the futility/casualty ratio. ARTILLERY (D74 universal-combat principle): the crest batteries are modeled on the UNIVERSAL GUN MODEL — each carries a real GUN COUNT (guns) + realistic crew (men), and its fire derives from guns * FLDA.GUN_FIRE_WEIGHT, the SAME per-gun weight in every battle (no per-battle 'volume-of-fire proxy men' fudge, which the C-1/D73 build used and D74 removed). The Confederate defensive advantage EMERGES from scenario DESIGN — the stone wall's cover, the massed historical gun count crowning the heights, and the cautious-attacker posture dying exposed on the open glacis — re-tuned via geometry/OOB, not by inflating strength.

weather.note: Cold, clear day; the morning river-valley fog over the Rappahannock and the town lifted in mid-morning (~10 a.m.), before the noon-to-dark assaults on Marye's Heights.
sources: Frank A. O'Reilly, The Fredericksburg Campaign: Winter War on the Rappahannock (LSU Press, 2003)
Fredericksburg & Spotsylvania National Military Park (NPS), 'Battle of Fredericksburg History: Marye's Heights' (nps.gov/frsp; Sumner's first assault began near noon, attacks continued until dark)
American Battlefield Trust, 'Battle of Fredericksburg' battle page (battlefields.org)

fredericksburg.name: Fredericksburg — the Assault on Marye's Heights
fredericksburg.place: Marye's Heights, Fredericksburg, Spotsylvania County, Virginia
fredericksburg.blurb: Burnside's Army of the Potomac storms uphill from the town, across an open plain cut by a canal, toward the sunken Telegraph Road — where Longstreet's men crouch behind a four-foot stone wall at the foot of Marye's Heights, the crest above crowned with pre-sighted guns. Seven Union divisions are fed in piecemeal for some fourteen separate charges; in history none reached the wall, and ~8,000 men fell on the glacis before it. Cross the millrace, climb the open swale, and try to do what the real Army of the Potomac could not.
fredericksburg.provenance: Verified (high) for the wave order, commanders, terrain transect, and the futility/casualty ratio; Inferred (medium) for per-brigade strengths and exact arrival minutes. The count of charges is a genuine historiographical spread — Wikipedia/American Battlefield Trust say 14 (seven divisions); NPS calls Hawkins's 'the fifteenth such Federal unit to charge the Sunken Road'; looser accounts give 16+. Sources: Wikipedia 'Battle of Fredericksburg' + Union/Confederate OOB (drawn from the Official Records); NPS Fredericksburg & Spotsylvania (the Sunken Road / Marye's Heights pages); American Battlefield Trust; USGS Circular 1264 (terrain); Frank O'Reilly, 'The Fredericksburg Campaign.' Anti-Lost-Cause framing: the lesson is a DOOMED FRONTAL ASSAULT into prepared defensive fire and the cost of piecemeal command — NOT Confederate invincibility. Lee won a defensive battle handed to him by a delayed-pontoon gamble and by Burnside's persistence after surprise was gone ('It is well that war is so terrible...').
fredericksburg._assaultDoctrineNote: The AI Union attacker fights the DOOMED FRONTAL ASSAULT — it advances in line across the open glacis and trades fire, but never column-rushes the killing ground or presses the mass bayonet on the covered sunken-road line (Burnside's piecemeal disaster, not the B-1 concentration doctrine). So the AI never carries the wall; the stone wall + pre-sighted crest guns repel it, the historical result. A HUMAN commanding the Union is unbound by this — the alt-history hook: do what the Army of the Potomac could not. Default fog OFF: the slaughter was in clear fields of fire, so 'fog aids the defender' is NOT the lever here — the cautious posture + the wall cover are.

menu.title: FREDERICKSBURG (1862)
menu.deck: The assault on Marye's Heights, brigade scale — command either side: storm the sunken road across the open glacis as Burnside's army, or hold the four-foot stone wall for Longstreet as charge after charge breaks before it.
menu.aria: Fredericksburg, December 1862 — the real-time historical battle. Command either army: lead the doomed Union assault across the open plain toward the sunken road and the stone wall, or hold the wall for the Confederacy as fourteen charges break before it.

sides.heading: Fredericksburg — Take Which Command?
sides.intro: December 13, 1862 · Burnside's grand assault on Marye's Heights. Lead the army of your choosing.

US.title: Lead the Union assault
US.deck: Form in the town, cross the canal under fire, and storm the open glacis toward the sunken road — and try to do what the Army of the Potomac could not.

CS.title: Hold for the Confederacy
CS.deck: Crouch your line behind the stone wall in the sunken road and rake the open ground; bring the crest guns and your reserves to bear as the charges come on.

sides.foot: The other army is commanded by the AI. The morning fog has lifted — clear fields of fire over open ground (press V in battle to toggle fog).

brief.attack: Cross the millrace and climb the open, rising glacis into the fire of the wall and the crest guns. This is your war: in December 1862 some fourteen charges broke here and none reached the wall — carry it, and you rewrite the slaughter.
brief.defend: Hold the sunken road behind the four-foot wall and let the open ground do its work — every charge must cross it under your fire and the pre-sighted guns above. This is your war: make the glacis a killing field, as Longstreet's men did.

objective.name: The Sunken Road & Stone Wall
objective.note: The sunken Telegraph Road behind its 4-foot stone wall at the base of Marye's Heights — the defender's line. The control CENTER sits just BEHIND the wall (z 331) so the defender doctrine's forward face (obj.z + obj.r*DEF_FACE_FRAC) lands the holding line exactly ON the wall (z ~403) in cover, the way Bull Run's objective seats its defenders on the Robinson-House fence — the wall is the forward face of the position, not a feature behind it. Historically no Union soldier held it; for a HUMAN Union player, seizing and HOLDING this line means clearing the whole sunken-road position (the wall line AND the guns sited just behind it), a high-cost alt-history lodgment the AI assault never achieves; the Confederate denies it to the clock. (Verified line; the Union taking it is alt-history.)

leaders._note: fate = a per-leader durability multiplier weighting the casualty model to HISTORY: >1 endures the day, <1 is fall-prone. At Marye's Heights only Cobb became a field-officer casualty among those modeled (mortally wounded); the others survived, so they fall only in a rare alt-history turn and never narrate a death as historical fact.

US.name: Maj. Gen. Edwin V. 'Bull' Sumner
US.note: Right Grand Division (II + IX Corps). His wing's 'secondary' attack on the stone wall became the main slaughter. Burnside kept him sending divisions in; Sumner survived the war's end. (Verified)
US.teach: Gen. Sumner is hit and carried from the town front — the assault loses its driving hand.

US.name: Maj. Gen. Winfield Scott Hancock
US.note: 1st Division, II Corps — the heaviest single-division loss of the battle (~2,032). His brigades (Zook, the Irish Brigade, Caldwell) reached the closest sustained ground. He survived. (Verified)
US.teach: Gen. Hancock is struck down — the hardest-driving division on the field falters.

US.name: Brig. Gen. Andrew A. Humphreys
US.note: 3rd Division, V Corps — the dusk bayonet charge with unloaded rifles; two horses were shot under him and he came through. (Verified)
US.teach: Gen. Humphreys's horse is shot from under him — and he presses the charge on foot into the dusk.

US.name: Brig. Gen. Nathan Kimball
US.note: Led French's first charge; severely wounded in the leg ~125 yards from the wall as his brigade was cut down (~25% loss). He survived the wound. (Verified)
US.teach: Gen. Kimball goes down wounded short of the wall — the first charge loses its leader on the glacis.

CS.name: Lt. Gen. James Longstreet
CS.note: First Corps, holding Marye's Heights. His engineer's eye for the sunken road and the artillery on the crest made the position a killing machine; E. P. Alexander told him 'a chicken could not live on that field.' He survived to 1904. (Verified)
CS.teach: Gen. Longstreet is unhorsed on the heights — the steadying hand behind the wall is lost.

CS.name: Brig. Gen. Thomas R. R. Cobb
CS.note: Commanded the wall sector at the outset. Mortally wounded near the Stephens house — a femoral artery severed; he bled to death December 13. The cause is contested (artillery shrapnel preponderance vs a rifle ball vs friendly fire); his brigadier's commission was still unconfirmed at his death. (Verified death; contested cause)
CS.teach: Gen. Cobb is mortally struck at the Stephens house — the wall's first commander is carried away dying, and the Georgians close ranks behind the stone.

CS.name: Brig. Gen. Joseph B. Kershaw
CS.note: Brought his South Carolina brigade DOWN from the crest to thicken the firing line at the wall after Cobb fell — the line stacked from two ranks to four. He survived the war. (Verified)
CS.teach: Gen. Kershaw rides the line at the wall — the firing ranks stack deeper and the volume of fire redoubles.

US.name: Union train — the pontoon crossings & the town

CS.name: Confederate train — the Telegraph Road behind the heights

hills.name: Marye's Heights
hills.note: The crest above the wall (Brompton, the Marye house). Only ~40-50 ft above the plain — its lethality was the wall at its base plus the pre-sighted artillery on top, not its height. (Verified)

hills.name: Willis Hill
hills.note: The southern portion of the heights, where Getty's final twilight assault struck along the unfinished railroad. (Verified)

hills.name: Stansbury / Taylor's Hill
hills.note: The northern continuation of the heights chain anchoring the Confederate left above the wall. (Verified)

woods.note: The shallow SWALE — the only meaningful cover on the plain above the canal; Union infantry sheltered here before crossing the open, gently-rising glacis (the final ~80 yards to the wall, z 403-485, are open killing ground under the wall and the crest guns). (Verified)
Timber along the southern heights / Willis Hill flank.
Timber along the northern heights flank.

walls.note: THE STONE WALL of the sunken Telegraph Road — a 4-foot wall fronting a roadbed cut below grade, a ready-made breastwork giving near-total cover for the firing line (log breastworks + abatis in places). ~480-500 yards long on the Union-facing side. This is the defender's hold line. (Verified)

markers.name: the canal / millrace
markers.note: A 15-ft-wide, water-filled (iced) ditch ~200 yd west of the town, crossable only at three partly-destroyed street bridges — it funneled the assault into vulnerable columns. (Verified)

markers.name: Hanover Street bridge
markers.note: The central canal crossing on the axis of advance toward the wall. (Verified)

markers.name: William Street bridge
markers.note: A southern canal crossing the assault funneled through. (Verified)

markers.name: Prussia Street bridge
markers.note: A northern canal crossing the assault funneled through. (Verified)

markers.name: Telegraph Road (the Sunken Road)
markers.note: The pre-war County Road, cut below grade — 'Sunken Road' is the post-battle name. The stone wall retains its eastern bank. (Verified)

markers.name: Hanover Street
markers.note: The central axis from the town across the canal to the foot of the heights. (Verified)

markers.name: the Rappahannock River
markers.note: The Union rear; the army crossed on pontoon bridges under the guns of Stafford Heights. The late pontoons forfeited the surprise. (Verified)

markers.name: Fredericksburg (the town)
markers.note: The Union marshalling ground; brigades formed in its streets under fire and crossed the canal onto the glacis.

markers.name: the open glacis — 'a chicken could not live on that field'
markers.note: ~300-400 yd of smooth, gently-rising open ground above the canal, raked by the wall and the crest guns. (Verified)

markers.name: to the Telegraph Road & the heights
markers.note: The Confederate rear behind Marye's Heights.

US.name: Kimball's Brigade
US.note: 1st Bde, French's 3rd Div, II Corps — the very first charge (~noon). Crossed the millrace on the narrow bridges, formed behind the swale, and was cut down ~125 yd from the wall (~25% loss, ~520 casualties per Mason's OR report); Kimball severely wounded. (Verified order; Inferred strength)

US.name: Andrews's Brigade
US.note: 3rd Bde, French's 3rd Div — followed Kimball up the glacis with Palmer's brigade and was broken on the same ground (~50% loss). Trailing-brigade order Inferred. (Verified that French's div opened the assault)

CS.name: Cobb's Brigade
CS.commander: Brig. Gen. Thomas R. R. Cobb
CS.note: McLaws's Div, Longstreet's First Corps: 16th/18th/24th Georgia + Cobb's & Phillips' Legions, crouched behind the stone wall in the sunken road (z 390, hard against the wall at z ~403 — within the cover radius). Veterans (xp3 — Longstreet's First Corps). Strength varies by source/definition (Wikipedia ~3,000 Georgians; ~2,000 on the wider McLaws front) — Inferred. Cobb mortally wounded near the Stephens house. (Verified identity; Inferred strength)

CS.name: Ransom's North Carolinians (at the wall)
CS.note: Ransom's division (the 24th/25th/35th/49th NC and Cooke's brigade) held the northern stretch of the sunken road behind the wall alongside Cobb's Georgians from the outset (z 390, in the wall's cover). (Verified - reframed to division/brigade scale: the prior name '24th North Carolina' put a lone regiment under a division commander.)

CS.name: Washington Artillery (New Orleans)
CS.note: The ~9 guns of the Washington Artillery, on the forward slope behind the sunken road (z 330, within canister reach of the open glacis), firing pre-sighted interlocking fire that raked the whole approach; held until ammunition ran low late in the day. Modeled on the UNIVERSAL GUN MODEL (D74): guns=9 (its fire derives from the gun count via the universal weight, the same as a gun anywhere else), men=~200 the realistic battalion crew (cannoneers + drivers + horse-holders) — NOT an inflated volume-of-fire proxy. (Verified position/role; gun count Verified ~9.)

CS.name: Marye's Hill Battery (the massed crest guns)
CS.note: The other guns crowning Marye's and Willis Hill — Maurin's, the Donaldsonville Artillery, and the batteries echeloned down the slope that, with the Washington Artillery, made the crest a continuous arc of fire over the sunken road. A historically-defensible aggregate of ~16 crest guns (the heights bristled with artillery covering the glacis). Universal gun model (D74): guns=16 drives its fire by the same per-gun weight as every battery in every battle; men=~350 the realistic crew of that many guns. (Verified that massed artillery crowned the heights; the single entry aggregates the echeloned batteries.)

reinforcements.name: Zook's Brigade
reinforcements.note: 3rd Bde, Hancock's 1st Div, II Corps — first of Hancock's brigades up the glacis, broken short of the wall. (Verified)

reinforcements.name: the Irish Brigade (Meagher's)
reinforcements.note: 2nd Bde, Hancock's div, ~1,200-1,315: the 69th/63rd/88th NY, 28th Mass, 116th Pa. Charged into the fire of Confederate Irishmen (Cobb's) and lost ~545 (~45%). Meagher, his knee injured, addressed and dispatched the regiments but did NOT reach the wall on foot. (Verified — do not narrate him at the wall)

reinforcements.name: Caldwell's Brigade
reinforcements.note: 1st Bde, Hancock's div. Col. Nelson Miles led two regiments to within ~40 yd of the wall — the closest any sustained line came — and was shot through the throat (survived); Caldwell hit twice. (Verified)

reinforcements.name: Kershaw's Brigade
reinforcements.entry: down from the crest to thicken the wall
reinforcements.note: 2nd/3rd/7th/8th/15th South Carolina + 3rd Bn. Came down off Marye's Heights after Cobb fell to reinforce the sunken road (detrains just behind the line, then crowds up to the wall) — the firing line stacked from two ranks to four, redoubling the fire. Veterans (xp3). (Verified)

reinforcements.name: Owen's Brigade
reinforcements.entry: Howard's division, diverted from the left
reinforcements.note: Philadelphia Brigade, Howard's 2nd Div, II Corps — diverted from a planned envelopment of the Confederate left and fed straight forward over the fallen; all repulsed. (Verified)

reinforcements.name: Cooke's Brigade
reinforcements.entry: Ransom's North Carolinians, up to the wall
reinforcements.note: 15th/27th/46th/48th NC of Ransom's division — crowded down to the wall in close succession with Kershaw to deepen the line on the northern stretch. Cooke wounded in the forehead. (Verified)

reinforcements.name: Sturgis's Division (Nagle & Ferrero)
reinforcements.note: 2nd Div, IX Corps — Nagle's and Ferrero's brigades committed in tandem with Howard but uncoordinated, fed piecemeal against the wall (~1,011 casualties). (Verified)

reinforcements.name: Alexander's Reserve Artillery
reinforcements.entry: relieving the Washington Artillery on the crest
reinforcements.note: ~9 guns (Woolfolk / Donnell Smith / Moody) relieved the Washington Artillery ~3 p.m. as its ammunition ran low, in time to help break Humphreys's charge — Alexander it was who told Longstreet 'a chicken could not live on that field.' On the forward slope (z 330) within canister reach of the glacis. Universal gun model (D74): guns=9, men=~200 realistic crew — not a volume-of-fire proxy. (Verified position/role; gun count Verified ~9.)

reinforcements.name: Griffin's Division (V Corps)
reinforcements.entry: Hooker's grand division renews the attack
reinforcements.note: 1st Div, V Corps — Barnes's, Sweitzer's, and Stockton's brigades smashed against the wall 'one by one' to relieve Sturgis. (Verified)

reinforcements.name: Humphreys's Division (the bayonet charge)
reinforcements.entry: a bayonet charge with UNLOADED rifles into the dusk
reinforcements.note: 3rd Div, V Corps — Allabach's and Tyler's brigades of green nine-month Pennsylvanians, sent on a false report the Confederates were retreating. Reached ~50 yd; division loss ~1,019 (~25%), Allabach's brigade ~581. (Verified)

reinforcements.name: Getty's Division (Hawkins's Brigade)
reinforcements.entry: along the unfinished railroad, at dusk
reinforcements.note: 3rd Div, IX Corps — the last assault, against Willis Hill on the Confederate left, approaching along the unfinished RF&P railroad in the gathering twilight (Hawkins's brigade, 'the fifteenth such Federal unit to charge the Sunken Road'); repulsed. (Verified)

US.US: History is overturned. Your assault did what the Army of the Potomac could not — it crossed the killing glacis and carried the sunken road. In December 1862 some fourteen charges broke before this wall and roughly 8,000 Union soldiers fell on the open ground in front of it.
US.CS: History is overturned. The Union assault carried the sunken road your men held — the stone wall that in 1862 stopped fourteen charges and cost the attackers some 8,000 men gave way under your defense.

CS.US: History holds. As in December 1862, the stone wall and the pre-sighted guns on Marye's Heights broke every charge; your assault joined the roughly 8,000 Union soldiers who fell on the open glacis before a wall no one reached.
CS.CS: History holds. As in December 1862, your line behind the stone wall in the sunken road broke charge after charge across the open plain; roughly 8,000 Union soldiers fell on the glacis and none reached the wall.

endNote.draw: A grinding, bloodier repulse than history's lopsided slaughter — but the stone wall still held the sunken road when the winter light failed.

teaching.title: The Slaughter Pen of the War

cards.head: Fourteen charges, and none reached the wall
cards.body: Seven Union divisions were fed up the glacis one or two brigades at a time — some fourteen separate charges (NPS counts a fifteenth unit). None reached the stone wall; the closest dead fell ~25-40 yards short. Roughly 8,000 Union soldiers fell in front of the wall against a fraction of that behind it. This was the rifle-musket and the entrenched defender at their deadliest: prepared cover and pre-sighted artillery against men crossing open ground. It is why a frontal assault on a ready position was, by 1862, a way to destroy your own army.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) — NPS Fredericksburg & Spotsylvania; American Battlefield Trust; the Official Records. ~8,000 Union before the wall firm; the ~1,200 Confederate-at-the-wall subset is single-source (Inferred at that granularity).

cards.head: The bridges that lost the battle
cards.body: Burnside's plan depended on pontoon bridges to cross the Rappahannock and seize the heights before Lee concentrated. The pontoons arrived weeks late; by the time the army crossed, Longstreet held Marye's Heights and Jackson the ground to the south. Burnside attacked frontally anyway. The lesson here is a command failure compounded by persistence after surprise was gone — not Confederate invincibility.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) — American Battlefield Trust; NPS; Frank O'Reilly, 'The Fredericksburg Campaign.'

cards.head: 'It is well that war is so terrible'
cards.body: Watching the assaults break against his line, Lee is said to have remarked to Longstreet, 'It is well that war is so terrible — we should grow too fond of it.' A lopsided defensive victory is the most seductive kind: it looks like genius and costs the winner little. But Fredericksburg settled nothing — Lee could not counterattack across the same open ground, and the war ground on. The victory's chief lesson was about the murderous new power of the defense, a lesson both armies would relearn at a far greater cost.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) for the battle's character; the exact quotation is traditional/attributed (Inferred wording). Sources: American Battlefield Trust; NPS.

cards.head: The wounded on the cold plain
cards.body: Thousands of wounded Union men lay on the frozen glacis through a bitter night, unreachable under Confederate fire; an aurora borealis is reported in the aftermath. Sgt. Richard Kirkland of South Carolina is remembered as 'the Angel of Marye's Heights' for carrying water to the enemy wounded over the wall — a deed documented after the war and debated in its details, but emblematic of the human cost the slaughter left on the field.
cards.provenance: Contested (low-med) in detail (the Kirkland story and the aurora date, Dec 13 vs 14, are disputed); Verified that the wounded froze on the field overnight. Sources: NPS; American Battlefield Trust.

codex.title: Fredericksburg — the Assault on Marye's Heights, December 13, 1862
codex.summary: Burnside's repeated frontal assaults across the open plain broke against Longstreet's stone-wall line at the foot of Marye's Heights; ~8,000 Union soldiers fell before a wall no charge reached. A one-sided Confederate defensive victory and the war's clearest lesson in the deadliness of prepared defense.
